quotes.verbatim_quote:
The tonic chord of a blues is a dominant 7 chord, a fact that doesn't fit very well
in traditional music theory.

¢ The blues is not only about chord changes and scales, but is also about
a certain sound, a feeling. Responsible for that sound are the blue
notes: a lowered 3rd note and a lowered 5th note.

* The 3 basic chords of a blues are all dominant 7 chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the core harmonic and tonal properties that distinguish blues from standard tonal music.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
those chords can be embellished with 9ths,
13ths, 6ths, and other color tones. So, if you hear or see a G13 chord and it's
written as G7, that's a common approach to comping over lead sheet jazz-blues
chord changes
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practical comping rule teaching players how to read jazz lead sheets and expand basic chord symbols with color tones.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Notice
how these changes use only three chords, the |7, V7 and V7 chords. Because of
this, they are often referred to as I-IV-V blues chord changes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the foundational terminology for the basic 12-bar blues form against which all later progressions are measured.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because there is a quick move to the IV7 chord and back to the tonic in the
first three bars, this chord progression is often referred to as a quick change
blues progression.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines 'quick change blues' and the harmonic move (IV7 in bar 2) that distinguishes the 1930s form from basic blues.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
One of the innovations Count Basie brought to the blues, or at least popularized,
is the use of the #IVdim7 chord in bars two and 6 of a jazz blues progression.

You will also see in the examples below that there is a iim7-V7/IV in bar 4 of the
tune, as well as a VI7b9 chord in bar 8, both now commonly used ideas that were
popularized by the Basie Band.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the specific harmonic substitutions (#IVdim7, iim7-V7/IV, VI7b9) associated with the Basie blues style.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Getting into the bebop era with these changes, you will notice a ii-V of the iim7
chord in bar 8, as well as a iii-VI-ii-V progression in the last bar of the tune, which
showcases the bebopper’s love of ii-V’s and fast moving changes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines structural hallmarks of bebop blues harmony — chain ii-V substitutions and turnaround cycles.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
* Bar 6: the Bb7 is replaced with a tritone ii-V (Bm7-E7).
¢ Bar 6 and 7: there are four descending 7th chords in bars 7 and 8, with the E7 and

Eb7 being used to connect F7 and D7b9 chromatically.
* Bar 10: there is a tritone approach chord added to the Am7, Bb7 in place of E7
(the V7 of Am7).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Bar-by-bar instructions for applying tritone substitution to specific locations in the blues form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Reflecting the bebop love of ii-V's, this progression is full of various ii-V progressions
in anumber of different keys.

A Bird blues starts and ends with an Fmaj7 chord, which is odd for a blues
progression.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the defining feature of Bird Blues — the Imaj7 tonic replacing the usual I7.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Bar 5-6: there’s some contrary motion going on here. The bass goes from c to c#
(upward motion), while the d of C9 goes to the c# (downward motion) of C#°7.
Contrary motion in voice leading sounds nice.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Concrete bar-specific example of contrary-motion voice leading stated as a preferred technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Bar 10: Sus chords are a nice way to delay and bring extra motion to dominant
chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Concise rule explaining sus chords' functional role in jazz comping as a rhythmic/harmonic tension device.

quotes.topic: Mixing b3 and 3 for bluesy sound
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The first 8 bars use the F minor pentatonic scale, mixed with the major 3rd (bar

3). Mixing b3 and 3 is often used by jazz musicians to create a bluesy sound. One

way to do this is mixing the F minor blues scale with the F Mixolydian scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the scale-mixing strategy (minor blues + Mixolydian) that generates the characteristic jazz-blues sound.

quotes.topic: Lydian Dominant as tritone sub scale
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Bar 16 uses the B Lydian Dominant scale (= F Altered scale). B7 is the tritone
substitute of F7 and creates an altered sound over F.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines Lydian Dominant's function as the scale choice over a tritone substitute chord, linking substitution theory to scale selection.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The classic lick in bar 20 uses the G Harmonic Minor scale over D7, creating a 7b9
sound:
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the rule for using harmonic minor over a dominant chord to generate the 7b9 color.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
one of the key elements to explore is mixing chords and single-
lines during each phrase of an improvised chorus, which you can hear during the
melody section of Chitlins especially.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the characteristic Burrell technique — interspersing three-note chord shapes with blues-scale single lines.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
1. Play the solo with a backing track over the first chorus. During the second
chorus you improvise the single lines but keep the chords as is. Continue this
alteration throughout the backing track.

2. Write out your own Kenny Burrell inspired solo over an A minor blues
progression, using the blues scale for the single notes, and small, three-note
chords for the comping sections of the solo.

3. Practice singing an improvised single-line where you see the single-notes in
the solo above, then comp the chords on the guitar where they land. This is a
great way to connect your ears and voice to your fretboard while working ona
minor blues at the same time.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Three structured practice protocols for internalizing chord-melody soloing, progressing from imitation to composition to ear-training.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Here is another example of a jazz blues solo (over a blues in G), this time in the
style of Joe Pass. In this example he mixes the minor and major pentatonic scales.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the defining technique of the Joe Pass solo example — minor/major pentatonic mixing.
